## TASK 3 — Effort-break L3 `lem:gf_splice_shortExact` into a 3-sub-lemma chain

137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.

199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300

Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple

41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. 1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	\leanok
47	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
48	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
49	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
50	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
51	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
52	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
53	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
54	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
55	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
56	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
57	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
58	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
59	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
60	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
61	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
62	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
63	
64	  The formal target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic} regards \(M\) as an
65	  \(A\)-module through the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\), and packages the
66	  localisation \(M_f\) as the localised module at the powers of \(f\): there exists
67	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M}
68	  is free over \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
69	\end{theorem}
70	
71	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
72	% exactly this signature):
73	%   (A B M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
74	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
75	%     [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
76	%     [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
77	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
78	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
79	
80	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
81	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
82	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
83	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
84	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
85	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
86	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
87	% there exists a finite filtration
88	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
89	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
90	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
91	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
92	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
93	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
94	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
95	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
96	\begin{proof}
97	
98	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case as a thin Mathlib wrapper.}
99	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself (i.e.\ \(M\) is a finite
100	  \(A\)-module, not merely a finite \(B\)-module). Since \(A\) is noetherian, a finite
101	  \(A\)-module is finitely presented over \(A\). Let \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) be the
102	  localisation of \(A\) at the multiplicative set \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\). The
103	  generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M = S^{-1} M\) is a \emph{finite-dimensional}
104	  \(K\)-vector space, hence free over \(K\); thus the freeness hypothesis at the
105	  generic localisation is automatic, with no input from the structure of \(B\). The
106	  result is then exactly Mathlib's
107	  \texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers}
108	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Free}): a finitely-presented \(A\)-module
109	  that is free over the localisation \(A_S = K\) becomes free over
110	  \texttt{Localization.Away}~\(r\) after inverting some \(r \in S\), i.e.\ some
111	  \(r \in A\), \(r \ne 0\). Setting \(f := r\) gives \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). (The
112	  openness of the free locus is the \texttt{Module.freeLocus} API of
113	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Spectrum.Prime.FreeLocus}, which records that the locus
114	  where \(M\) is free over \(A\) is open and, by the above, non-empty.) This closes
115	  the theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
116	
117	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
118	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
119	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
120	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
121	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
122	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
123	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
124	  for exactly this residue.
125	
126	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction), as a \texttt{\textbackslash uses}-linked chain.}
127	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
128	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
129	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
130	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
131	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
132	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
133	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
134	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
135	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
136	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
137	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
138	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
139	\end{proof}
140	
141	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
142	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
143	
144	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
145	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
146	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
147	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each
148	\texttt{\textbackslash uses} only earlier blocks (or Mathlib anchors). Throughout,
149	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
150	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
151	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
152	
153	\begin{lemma}\leanok
154	[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
155	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
156	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
157	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
158	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
159	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
160	\end{lemma}
161	
162	\begin{proof}
163	
164	\leanok
165	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction and is already closed in Lean
166	  axiom-clean. A finite module over a noetherian ring is finitely presented, so
167	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
168	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
169	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
170	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
171	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
172	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
173	  \(f := r\).
174	\end{proof}
175	
176	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
177	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
178	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
179	  \mathlibok
180	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
181	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
182	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
183	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
184	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r = \texttt{Localization.Away}~r\) (of the same finite
185	  rank).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{lemma}\leanok
189	[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
190	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
191	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
192	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
193	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
194	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
195	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
196	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
197	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
198	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
199	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
200	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
201	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
202	  induction).}
203	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
204	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
205	  \(A_f\).
206	\end{lemma}
207	
208	\begin{proof}
209	
210	\leanok
211	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
212	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
213	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
214	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
215	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
216	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
217	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
218	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
219	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
220	\end{proof}

222	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
223	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
224	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
225	  \mathlibok
226	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
227	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
228	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
229	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
230	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
231	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
232	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
233	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
234	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
235	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
236	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
237	\end{lemma}

241	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}

243	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark

254	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
255	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
256	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation
257	  \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).

260	\begin{proof}
261	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
262	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
263	  Localisation at the multiplicative set generated by \(f = f'f''\) is exact, so it
264	  carries the given short exact sequence to a short exact sequence
265	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules. Both ends are free over
266	  \(A_f\): \(M'_f\) is the further localisation of the free \(A_{f'}\)-module
267	  \(M'_{f'}\) at \(f''\) and \(M''_f\) likewise, and a localisation of a free module
268	  is free. A short exact sequence with free quotient \(M''_f\) splits, so
269	  \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\) as a direct sum of free
270	  modules.
271	\end{proof}

318	\begin{proof}
319	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
320	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
321	  Apply Noether normalisation over the field \(K\)
322	  (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
323	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
324	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
325	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
326	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)). Fix a finite
327	  set of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) and
328	  finitely many equations of integral dependence they satisfy; let \(g \in A\),
329	  \(g \ne 0\), be a common denominator of the (finitely many) coefficients of those
330	  equations. After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold over
331	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
332	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
333	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
334	\end{proof}
335	
336	\begin{lemma}\leanok
337	[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
338	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
340	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base}
341	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
342	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
343	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
344	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
345	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
346	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
347	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
348	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
349	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
350	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
351	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
352	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
353	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
354	  core of the induction).}
355	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
356	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
357	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
358	
359	  This is the genuine Mathlib-absent residue of generic freeness: the bottom of the
360	  Nitsure \S4 induction, on which the whole finite-type case rests.
361	\end{lemma}
362	
363	\begin{proof}
364	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
365	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
366	  We induct on \(d\). For \(d = 0\) the ring is \(A\) itself and \(N\) is a finite
367	  \(A\)-module, so the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
368	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}. For \(d \ge 1\), pass to \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
369	  set \(N_K = K \otimes_A N\). If \(N_K = 0\) the torsion base case
370	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} already produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\) free.
371	  Otherwise let \(m\) be the generic rank of \(N\) over the domain
372	  \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); choosing \(m\) elements of \(N\) whose images form a
373	  \(K(X_1, \dots, X_d)\)-basis of \(N_K\) and clearing denominators gives, after
374	  inverting some \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\), a short exact sequence of
375	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d]\)-modules
376	  \[
377	    0 \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d]^{\oplus m} \to N_g \to T \to 0
378	  \]
379	  with \(T\) a finite torsion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d]\)-module. Because \(T\) is
380	  torsion, its support over \(K[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) has dimension \(\le d - 1\), so
381	  \(T\) is finite over a polynomial ring in fewer variables after a further
382	  inversion; the inductive hypothesis yields \(h \ne 0\) with \(T_h\) free over
383	  \(A_{gh}\). The free middle term \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_d]^{\oplus m}\) localises to a
384	  free \(A_{gh}\)-module by the \(d = 0\) leaf applied coordinatewise, and
385	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the two ends of the localised sequence to
386	  give \(N_f\) free over \(A_f\) with \(f := gh\).
387	\end{proof}
388	
389	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
390	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
391	
392	The following two file-internal helpers in the \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace package
393	the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms (flatness and module-finiteness) that the
394	chain and the geometric globalization consume. Both are already proved axiom-clean.
395	
396	\begin{lemma}
397	
398	\leanok
399	[Flat localization away, finite module]
400	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
401	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
402	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
403	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
404	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} is
405	  \emph{flat} over \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
406	\end{lemma}
407	\begin{proof}
408	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
409	  \leanok
410	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} the localized module is free over
411	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}; free modules are flat (\texttt{Module.Flat.of\_free}).
412	\end{proof}
413	
414	\begin{lemma}
415	
416	\leanok
417	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
418	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
419	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
420	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
421	  % NOTE (iter-003 review): prose understates the actual Lean hypotheses. The Lean decl
422	  % takes B a module-finite A-algebra ([Module.Finite A B]) and M a finite B-module
423	  % ([Module.Finite B M]) with the scalar tower A → B → M; A-finiteness of M is DERIVED
424	  % (via Module.Finite.trans), not a hypothesis. It is strictly more general than a mere
425	  % repackaging of lem:gf_finite_module. Planner: dispatch a blueprint-writer to restate
426	  % the hypotheses (see task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-gf.md, major finding 2).
427	  The module-finiteness packaging of \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}: for \(A\) a noetherian
428	  domain and \(M\) a module that is finite as an \(A\)-module, there exists \(f \ne 0\)
429	  with \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M} free over
430	  \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
431	\end{lemma}
432	\begin{proof}
433	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
434	  \leanok
435	  Identical to \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}, packaged against the
436	  \texttt{Module.Finite} hypothesis.
437	\end{proof}
438	
439	
440	\begin{theorem}
441	
442	\leanok
443	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
444	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
445	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
446	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
447	  % NOTE (re-signed iter-002): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
448	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
449	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any
450	  % non-empty open). It was re-signed in iter-002 (the decl is NOT in
451	  % archon-protected.yaml) to carry the coherence encoding below; the Lean file is
452	  % now the source of truth for the exact binders and the full flatness conclusion.
453	  %
454	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a
455	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
456	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:
457	  %     [F.IsQuasicoherent]   (SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent,
458	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)
459	  %     [F.IsFiniteType]      (SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType,
460	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Generators)
461	  %
462	  % LEAN SIGNATURE HEADER (now landed in the .lean file):
463	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
464	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] (F : X.Modules)
465	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
466	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
467	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
468	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
469	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
470	
471	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
472	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
473	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
474	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
475	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
476	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
477	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
478	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
479	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
480	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
481	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
482	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
483	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
484	
485	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: without it the conclusion is
486	  false, since a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module need not become flat over any
487	  non-empty open of \(S\). The intended formal target therefore carries \(\F\)
488	  coherent over the locally noetherian base \(S\), which — absent a single
489	  \texttt{IsCoherent} predicate in Mathlib at this pin — is encoded as the
490	  conjunction of the two module-sheaf hypotheses \(\F\) quasi-coherent
491	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) and \(\F\) of finite type
492	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}), together with \(p\) locally of finite type
493	  and \(S\) integral and locally noetherian.
494	\end{theorem}
495	
496	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
497	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
498	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
499	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
500	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
501	% by restricting to a non-empty
502	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
503	\begin{proof}
504	  
505	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
506	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian; existence of such a \(U_0\)
507	  by \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset} applied at a point of the non-empty
508	  \(S\)). We assemble the witness \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
509	
510	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
511	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
512	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
513	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
514	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
515	
516	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
517	  of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the
518	  associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and
519	  finite type of \(\F\) (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}) makes \(M_j\) a finite
520	  \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is the data fed
521	  to the algebraic form.
522	
523	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
524	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
525	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
526	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
527	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty (basic) open; over \(V\) every
528	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\) (freeness is preserved by the further localization
529	  \(A_{f_j} \to A_f\)).
530	
531	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness \(\Rightarrow\) flatness, checked locally.} On each patch the
532	  localized section module is free, hence flat over \(A_f\) by
533	  \texttt{Module.Flat.of\_free}; the fibrewise flatness conclusion (flatness of
534	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) over \(\Gamma(S, U)\) for affine \(U \le V\), \(W \le p^{-1}U\)) then
535	  follows from these local freeness statements by the localize-at-maximal-ideals
536	  flatness criterion \texttt{Module.flat\_of\_isLocalized\_maximal}. This produces the
537	  required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
538	\end{proof}

540	\section{Mathlib status}
541	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
542	
543	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
544	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
545	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together

553	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
554	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
555	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
556	are introduced in

558	file-internal \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace already lands the

1706	\centerline{\large\bf Lemma on Generic Flatness}
1707	
1708	\medskip
1709	
1710	
1711	\begin{lemma} \label{algebraic lemma for generic flatness}
1712	Let $A$ be a noetherian domain, 
1713	and $B$ a finite type $A$ algebra. Let $M$ be a finite $B$-module. 
1714	Then there exists an $f\in A$, $f\ne 0$, such that 
1715	the localisation $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$.
1716	\end{lemma}
1717	
1718	
1719	\proof Over the quotient field $K$ of $A$, the $K$-algebra 
1720	$B_K = K\otimes _AB$ is of finite type, and $M_K = K\otimes_AM$ is a 
1721	finite module over $B_K$. Let $n$ be the dimension of the support
1722	of $M_K$ over $\spec B_K$. We argue by induction on $n$, starting
1723	with $n=-1$ which is the case when $M_K =0$. In this case, as
1724	$K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$, each $v\in M$
1725	is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
1726	generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating 
1727	elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$.
1728	Hence $M_f =0$, proving the lemma when $n=-1$.
1729	
1730	Now let $n\ge 0$, and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
1731	As $B$ is noetherian and $M$ is assumed to be a finite $B$-module,
1732	there exists a finite filtration
1733	$$0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M$$
1734	where each $M_i$ is a $B$-submodule of $M$ such that for each $i\ge 1$
1735	the quotient module $M_i/M_{i-1}$ is isomorphic to $B/\pp_i$ for 
1736	some prime ideal $\pp_i$ in $B$. 
1737	
1738	Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact
1739	sequence of $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of
1740	$A$ such that $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over
1741	respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$, then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$
1742	where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact repeatedly.
1743	Therefore it is enough to prove the result when 
1744	$M$ is of the form $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. 
1745	This reduces us to the case where $B$ is a domain and $M=B$.
1746	
1747	As by assumption $K\otimes_AB$ has dimension $n\ge 0$ (that is,
1748	$K\otimes_AB$ is non-zero), the map $A\to B$ must be injective.
1749	By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements 
1750	$b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$, 
1751	such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra
1752	$K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and 
1753	the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent
1754	over $K$. (For simplicity of notation, we write $1\otimes b$ simply as $b$.)
1755	If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for  
1756	coefficients of equations of integral dependence 
1757	satisfied by a finite set of
1758	algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, 
1759	we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. 
1760	
1761	Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
1762	domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
1763	sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1764	$$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
1765	where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$.
1766	Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
1767	as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
1768	induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$), 
1769	there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$.
1770	Taking $f=gh$, the lemma follows from the 
1771	above short exact sequence.
1772	\hfill$\square$
1773	
1774	
1775	
1776	The above theorem has the following consequence, which follows
1777	by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of $S$.
1778	
1779	
1780	
1781	\begin{theorem}\label{generic flatness} 
1782	Let $S$ be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1783	Let $p: X\to S$ be a finite type morphism, and let $\F$ be a 
1784	coherent sheaf of ${\mathcal O}_X$-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1785	open subscheme $U\subset S$ such that the restriction of $\F$ 
1786	to $X_U = p^{-1}(U)$ is flat over ${\mathcal O}_U$.
1787	\end{theorem}

\textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
  normalisation with denominators).}
  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Then there exist
  \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and elements \(b_1, \dots, b_n \in B\), algebraically
  independent over \(K\), such that the localisation \(B_g\) is module-finite over
  its polynomial subalgebra \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
\end{lemma}
input.new_string:
\textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
  normalisation with denominators).}
  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
  \(A_g = \texttt{Localization.Away}~g\) and
  \(B_g = \texttt{Localization.Away (algebraMap } A\,B\,g)\) for the localisations
  away from \(g\) and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element
  \(g \in A\) with \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
  \[
    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
  \]
  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] = \texttt{MvPolynomial (Fin } n)~A_g\) is the
  polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is
  module-finite over the image polynomial subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity
  of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic independence of the generators
  \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and the module-finiteness is taken over the algebra
  structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\). Equivalently, the classical statement
  ``\(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)'' is encoded by the single map
  \(\varphi\): it both names the polynomial subalgebra (as the domain
  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)) and carries the algebra structure under which \(B_g\) is
  finite.
\end{lemma}

input.new_string:
\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra
  (\texttt{[Module.Finite A B]}), and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module
  (\texttt{[Module.Finite B M]}) with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the
  scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such
  that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers} f) M\) is free over
  \texttt{Localization.Away} f\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
  The \(A\)-finiteness of \(M\) is \emph{derived}, not assumed: since \(B\) is
  module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\), composition along
  the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\) makes \(M\) module-finite over \(A\) (transitivity
  of module-finiteness, \texttt{Module.Finite.trans}~\(B~M\)). With \(M\) now a finite
  \(A\)-module, \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} produces the required \(f \ne 0\) with
  \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers} f) M\) free over
  \texttt{Localization.Away} f\). The lemma is therefore strictly more general than a
  repackaging of \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}: the finite \(A\)-algebra \(B\) and the
  finite \(B\)-module \(M\) are the genuine inputs, and \(A\)-finiteness is a
  consequence of the hypotheses rather than one of them.
\end{proof}
